content:
10 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size A few weeks into learning JavaScript, I had a thought every self-taught developer has at some point: “Can I just skip ahead to React already?” DOM selectors felt tedious. Event listeners felt repetitive. And reading about XMLHttpRequest when I knew fetch() existed felt like learning to use a rotary phone. I’m glad I didn’t skip it. Here’s everything that changed my mind — the DOM, events, a debugging story I’m still a little proud of, and the road from callbacks to async/await — told through the actual code and actual mistakes that got me there. 1. The DOM Is Not Your HTML File — It’s a Living Tree The first mental shift was realizing JavaScript never actually touches your HTML file. The browser reads your markup once and builds a live tree of objects in memory — the DOM (Document Object Model). document is the root object you talk to in order to read or change anything on the page. Everything you "change" with JavaScript is just editing that in-memory tree, which the browser then repaints on screen. Six ways to select something, but really just two you’ll use javascript document.getElementById("id") // single element, by unique ID document.getElementsByClassName("cls") // HTMLCollection (live), by class document.getElementsByTagName("tag") // HTMLCollection (live), by tag document.getElementsByName("name") // NodeList, by the name attribute document.querySelector("css-selector") // single element, first match document.querySelectorAll("css-selector") // NodeList, every matchquerySelector and querySelectorAll quickly became my defaults — they accept any real CSS selector (.class, #id, div > p, input[type="text"]), while the older getElementsBy... methods only let you filter by one criterion at a time. And a selector that matches nothing doesn't throw an error — it just returns an empty collection. textContent vs innerText vs innerHTML This one tripped me up until I had a concrete example: an

with visible text plus a hidden inside it. javascript title.textContent // raw text, INCLUDING hidden content — ignores CSS, fastest title.innerText // only text actually visible on screen — respects CSS title.innerHTML // the literal HTML markup as a string, tags includedtitle.style.backgroundColor = "Red" // camelCase, not "background-color" title.innerText = "Updated heading" title.innerHTML = "Updated" title.setAttribute("id", "main-heading") title.classList.add("highlight")CSS property names become camelCase in JavaScript (backgroundColor, not background-color) because a hyphen in a property name would be read as subtraction. Walking the tree instead of re-querying every time Once you have one element, you can move relative to it instead of writing a new selector from scratch: javascript el.children // all element children (skips text/whitespace nodes) el.childNodes // all children, including text/comment nodes el.firstElementChild / el.lastElementChild el.parentElement el.nextElementSibling / el.previousElementSibling.children only counts actual elements — it silently skips whitespace, which is exactly why it's almost always what you want when looping over a list: javascript const parent = document.querySelector(".parent") for (let i = 0; i < parent.children.length; i++) { parent.children[i].style.color = "cyan" }The bug that’s actually worth knowing about: live vs static This is, hands down, the most-tested DOM concept, and the one most likely to bite you in a real app. TypeBehaviorHTMLCollection (from getElementsBy...)Live — re-checks the page every time you read itNodeList (from querySelectorAll)Static — a frozen snapshot from the moment you called it If you cache a collection early — think a live to-do list — and elements get added or removed afterward, an HTMLCollection silently reflects the change, while a NodeList does not. That's a classic, hard-to-spot source of "why isn't my list updating" bugs. (Fun terminology trap: childNodes is actually a live NodeList, so "NodeList = always static" isn't 100% universal — querySelectorAll specifically gives you a static one.) Neither one is a real array, either: javascript document.querySelectorAll(".item").forEach(item => item.style.color = "orange") // works// document.getElementsByClassName("item").forEach(...) // throws — HTMLCollection has no forEachconst realArray = Array.from(document.getElementsByClassName("item")) realArray.map(/* ... */).filter(/* ... */) // now any array method worksBuilding elements that don’t exist yet javascript const div = document.createElement("div") // Step 1: exists only in memory div.className = "main" div.id = Math.round(Math.random() * 10 + 1) div.style.backgroundColor = "Cyan"const addtext = document.createTextNode("Hey its me Rafay khan") div.appendChild(addtext) // Step 3: give it contentdocument.body.appendChild(div) // Step 4: THIS is what makes it visiblecreateElement() only builds the element in memory — nothing shows up until you appendChild() it into something already on the live page. Wrapped as a reusable function, this is the exact pattern behind any "add item" button: javascript function addlanguage(langName) { const li = document.createElement("li") li.textContent = `${langName}` document.querySelector(".language").appendChild(li) } addlanguage("python") addlanguage("goLang")Editing, replacing, and removing javascript // Swap an element entirely const secondlang = document.querySelector("li:nth-child(2)") const newLi = document.createElement("li") newLi.textContent = "mojo" secondlang.replaceWith(newLi)// Or overwrite directly with a raw HTML string document.querySelector("li:last-child").outerHTML = "
  • TypeScript
    • "// Remove, the modern one-liner document.querySelector("li:nth-child(2)").remove()innerHTML replaces what's inside a tag, keeping the tag itself. outerHTML replaces the whole element, tag and all, as if a brand-new one took its exact place in the tree. 2. Events: One Listener Can Do the Job of a Hundred My first instinct when building a to-do list was to attach a click listener to every single item. It worked, but it didn’t feel right. javascript document.getElementById("deathnote").addEventListener("click", function (e) { console.log("deathnote is clicked") }, false)The old style — element.onclick = function(){...} — still works, but only lets you attach one handler per event type per element; a second assignment silently overwrites the first. addEventListener lets you stack as many listeners as you want, which is why it's the standard today. The event object is doing more than you’d think PropertyWhat it tells youe.typeThe kind of event ("click", "keydown", ...)e.targetThe exact element the event originated one.clientX / e.clientYMouse position relative to the viewporte.altKey / e.ctrlKey / e.shiftKeyWhether a modifier key was helde.keyWhich key was pressed (the modern, readable version) Bubbling vs capturing, and the two methods people mix up Events don’t just affect the element they fire on — they travel through the tree. Bubbling (target → upward through every ancestor) is the default and the one you’ll use constantly. javascript document.getElementById("images").addEventListener("click", function () { console.log("image is clicked") })document.getElementById("deathnote").addEventListener("click", function (e) { console.log("deathnote is clicked") e.stopPropagation() // stop it from bubbling further up }, false)Without stopPropagation(), clicking the inner element would also trigger the outer listener, because the click bubbles upward past it on the way to document. This is where I used to mix things up constantly: javascript document.getElementById("google").addEventListener("click", function (e) { e.preventDefault() // stop the BROWSER'S default behavior (link navigating) e.stopPropagation() // stop the event from BUBBLING to parent listeners })Think of it this way: an event occurs → the browser has a default reaction queued up → preventDefault() says "skip your built-in reaction, I'll handle this." It has nothing to do with parent elements — that's stopPropagation()'s entire job. Delegation: the production-grade version of what I was doing manually javascript document.querySelector("#images").addEventListener("click", function (e) { console.log(e.target.parentNode.tagName) if (e.target.tagName === "IMG") { e.target.parentNode.remove() } })One listener on a parent, branching on e.target, scales far better than one listener per item — especially for lists that grow dynamically. New items added later are automatically covered, with zero extra listeners attached. 3. The Debugging Story That Taught Me More Than Any Tutorial This is the part I actually want to highlight, because it’s the most useful thing in this whole journey — and it didn’t come from getting something right. I was building a simple start/stop timer using setInterval and clearInterval. My first attempts failed, and I left myself this note directly in the code: mistakes i made 1. Declared startIntervel inside the Start callback — the Stop button couldn't see it. 2. Used const for startIntervel, then tried to assign a new interval ID to it. 3. Called clearInterval() without passing the interval ID.Three mistakes, three completely different categories of bug: • A scope bug. A variable declared inside one function is invisible to a sibling function. The fix is hoisting the declaration to a shared outer scope. • A const-reassignment bug. const cannot be reassigned, full stop. If a value needs to change later — like a timer ID created on demand — it has to be let. • A “forgot the argument” bug. clearInterval() does nothing useful without the specific ID it's meant to cancel. The working version, by comparison, looks almost boring: javascript let startIntervel // declared in the OUTER scope — this is the actual fixdocument.querySelector("#start").addEventListener("click", function () { if (!startIntervel) { // guard against stacking intervals on repeat clicks startIntervel = setInterval(function () { console.log(document.querySelector("#raf").innerHTML) }, 100) } })document.querySelector("#stop").addEventListener("click", function () { clearInterval(startIntervel) })setInterval keeps firing forever, on a fixed delay, until something calls clearInterval() with its ID. Unlike setTimeout, there's no automatic "done" state — lose the ID, and the interval keeps running in the background indefinitely. There’s also a leftover comment in my own notes wondering “idk why arrow function was not working” next to a commented-out setInterval(() => {...}, 2000) call. Worth closing that loop: arrow functions work completely normally as setInterval/setTimeout callbacks — there's no special restriction there. If it looked broken, the surrounding code block was almost certainly commented out at the time. Good instinct to question it, but the syntax was never the actual problem. 4. Understanding XHR Made fetch() Stop Feeling Like Magic Before fetch() existed, XMLHttpRequest (XHR) was the only way to send and receive data from JavaScript without reloading the page. It's verbose by today's standards, but fetch() is built directly on top of it — which means understanding it makes the modern version feel far less like a black box. javascript const req = new XMLHttpRequest() req.open("GET", "https://api.github.com/users/rafayykhan") // configure, doesn't send yetreq.onreadystatechange = function () { if (req.readyState === 4) { // 4 = fully complete const data = JSON.parse(this.responseText) // arrives as a STRING console.log(data.followers) } }req.send() // THIS actually fires itonreadystatechange fires on every state change, not just at the end — which is why the readyState === 4 guard isn't optional. Without it, the callback runs on states 0 through 4, and tries to JSON.parse an incomplete or empty response partway through. readyStateMeaning0UNSENT1OPENED2HEADERS_RECEIVED3LOADING4DONE — responseText is r
